text:
Can upgrading your PC make it worse? 👀

More cores, higher clocks, and a bigger price tag don't always mean better performance.

The 9950X loses to the 9800X3D in gaming thanks to 3D V-Cache.

A 6400 MT/s RAM stick sounds faster... until you check the CL36 latency vs CL30.

And double the capacity? Useless if it slows down your speeds.

Always read beyond the spec sheet.

The most breakable part in your PC? The motherboard. 😬

There are two moments where things go wrong fast.

First: installing the CPU. One wrong move and those tiny pins bend, and your PC is done.

Second: mounting the board in the case. A misplaced standoff or sliding it in at the wrong angle can snap the traces on the back, instantly turning it into a very expensive brick.

Handle with care. Seriously. 🛠️

The PS5 just got its biggest price hike ever. So we built a PC that beats it. 💀

Intel i5-12400F, a budget chipset mobo that's actually decent now, 16GB DDR4, a 1TB SSD, a Thermaltake case, a 500W PSU, and an RTX 3070 to top it all off.

The result? A build that goes head-to-head with the PS5 at its new price... and wins where it counts.

Sony really did us a favor.

The world's first programmable universal remote, designed by Steve Wozniak himself. 🤯

Back in 1987, you could program it to turn on your cable box, change the channel, start the VCR, record a show, and shut everything off, all with one sequence.

Today? It can pause Netflix, dim the lights, and open YouTube. One button = bathroom break mode.

Built for the highly educated, low-level programmer geek.

And honestly, no one else. 😂

RISC-V might be the most important chip architecture you've never heard of. 👀

Like ARM, it's a reduced instruction set architecture: efficient, low power, and great for wearables and small devices.

But unlike ARM, it's completely open source.

That's a massive deal for companies and countries locked out of x86 or ARM due to trade restrictions.

No license fees. No gatekeepers.

The future of chips might be open to everyone.

This might be the coolest gaming setup ever built. 🎮

A 16-core Ryzen CPU on a Mini-ITX motherboard, crammed inside a NES chassis.

Swap your entire OS by swapping a cartridge, each one hiding an NVMe SSD inside.

An external GPU linked via GPIO, living inside a retro CRT-style modded monitor.

Bunny antennas. A whale mascot.

This isn't a PC.

It's a work of art.

Liquid metal leaked inside my ASUS ROG Flow X13. Here's what happened. 😰

Liquid metal can absorb into solder when it melts, making it brittle and causing components to literally fall off the board.

We scraped, wiped with solvent, used a hot air station at the lowest possible temp, and swapped in PTM 7950.

The result? Couldn't fully save it BUT somehow ended up with slightly better thermals and a higher CPU clock.

Would I recommend liquid metal?

After this? Hard no.

That 16TB external SSD for $15 on Amazon? It's fake. 🚨

The drive shows 2TB of space... it's actually 64GB.

It's USB 2.0 disguised as 3.1.

The internals are hot glued.

And when it fills up? It just deletes your old files to make room.

As for the sellers: they use gibberish brand names so when they get banned, they change one letter, file a new trademark, and they're back.

Amazon has decided it's cheaper to compensate victims than to prevent the scam.

Let that sink in.

We built a jank version of the Valve Steam Machine to guess its price. 🤔

Ryzen 5 8400F, SODIMM RAM, a 500GB SSD, AMD stock cooler capped at 30W, RX 6600, and a Seasonic 550W PSU (because good 300W units basically don't exist).

Add a case and a GameSir controller.

And our best estimate for the real thing? $699 USD.

Unless the RAM market gets even worse.

Valve, please don't prove us wrong. 🙏

A random guy on the CES show floor built a better Apple mouse than Apple ever has. 😐

USB-C charging (not on the bottom).

A single button that works as left AND right click with haptic feedback.

And a proper power button.

It's the old-school Magic Mouse shell, completely reimagined and actually usable.

Apple, take notes.

Or don't. We know how this goes.
